title: [char] cast overflow: supplementary Unicode codepoints exceed System.Char 16-bit limit
description: PowerShell [char] type is System.Char (16-bit, max 0xFFFF / 65535). Codepoints above U+FFFF (supplementary planes: emoji, symbols) such as 0x1F50D (128269 decimal, magnifying glass) throw 'Cannot convert value to type System.Char: Value was either too large or too small for a character'. This pattern silently passes syntax checking but crashes at runtime when the cast is evaluated.
rootCause: [char] is a .NET System.Char (UTF-16 code unit). Supplementary Unicode characters (U+10000 to U+10FFFF) require surrogate pairs and cannot be represented by a single [char]. Developers copy emoji codepoint values from Unicode charts without realising the 16-bit limit.
resolution: Option A: Remove the [char] cast and use a UTF-8 string literal directly (e.g. '🔍'). Option B: Use [char]::ConvertFromUtf32(0x1F50D) which returns a surrogate pair string. Option C: Replace with a BMP-safe alternative character (e.g. 0x2315 for search icon).
preventionRule: SCAN: Any [char] cast with value > 0xFFFF (65535 decimal) is invalid. Regex: \[char\]\s*(0x[1-9a-fA-F][0-9a-fA-F]{4,}|[6-9]\d{4}|[1-9]\d{5,}). REMEDY: Replace with [char]::ConvertFromUtf32() or a BMP string literal.
detection_method: Runtime error log analysis: Write-AppLog at Main-GUI.ps1:4364 captured 'Cannot convert value 128269 to type System.Char'

fix_applied.description: Removed dead [char]0x1F50D assignment at line 2196 which was immediately overwritten by 'Filter:' string on line 2197. The emoji cast was unreachable output but threw before the overwrite could execute.
fix_applied.pattern: Remove [char] casts > 0xFFFF; use [char]::ConvertFromUtf32() or BMP alternatives
